item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: paper and wood are organic

item.title: Forbiden books?
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't there be protections so that O5's books aren't easily accessed? And what about all the foundation agents and random civilians that were exposed to text-based cognitohazards, infohazards etc?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The overall concept is great. It is executed very well and enjoyable. Definetly one of my favorite piece.

One thing that bothers me is the usage of carbon dating here. Shouldn't carbon dating only work on organic materials? Or does this imply that the books themselves are organic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this a neat concept, very enjoyable and a few questions were raises. I like that the Library gave Marvin and Molly their own books particularly.

Also curious what would happen if you added a biography of a fully functional character To the shelves? Would it go the way the Foundation's test with their fictional man did? Or would something more interesting happen if we slipped in a copy of "Tarzan Alive", "Sherlock Holmes of Baker Street" or "Doc Savage: His Apocalyptic Life"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly one of my favorite skips, up there with 3999 and 2000. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how I avoided reading this for so long, but I have to say I love the concept, execution, and implications of this piece. Outstanding, to be honest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember reading something somewhere which suggested that 049 used to be human but isn't anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could be used as a source of information on human scps, and information on them could certainly help countain them, not only that but on the object class guide it says that thaumiel class scps either help countain other anomalies or otherwise are fundamental to the foundations mission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the level 5 credential collapsible, why is there redacted data like the date? Don't O5 have to know everything?

item.title: LUKElcs
item.content:encoded: At first I thought the containment procedures over the top for whatever it was, but after reading the description I can understand why this would have to be kept so securely. I mean, one wrong move and you can change all of history. If the wrong guy got in there he could wright that Hitler took over the world, then it would come true and Hitler would rule the world. Not fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know I see what you mean, but I loved this entry.

I mean it was written really well, the narrative was somewhat comprehensive and touching, and it was believable (as believable as an SCP can be at least).

I'd easily say this is one of my favorite entries and the only thing I wish when finished with this is that there was more material

item.title: +1'ed
item.content:encoded:
So, when we gonna throw Able inside in case of emergency?
A perfect mission for him, if you ask me. I mean, molten iron will stop him after.
In Ressurection canon someone could even agree with it! I would for sure.

Now, weaponizing Able jokes aside, I read this back in August, but apparently I was stupid and didn't upvoted this beautiful thing. Done down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, this is one of my favorite scps. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this enough to sacrifice it's 420 status just to +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those skips that had me in a damn trance the more I read into it. I really like this one.

+1

item.title: Re: +400
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, holy crap. +400.

I must get around to some 'Tales from Alexandria' soon.

I somehow missed this during the contest, but am happy to push it over the milestone now. Excellently written, even if there are some minor logical inconsistencies that have been pointed out already.

I wonder what the absurdly-redacted in-universe subdocument about the current O5's books says…

item.title: Paradoxes in Retrocausality
item.content:encoded:
First off: Even if it fits the "Put it in a box" definition of Safe, I think classifying an anomaly that can (and has) retroactively rewrite reality in a way that the Foundation can't even detect as "Safe" is a hell of an understatement.

+ Besides that, the testing logs are terribly inconsistent.
– hide block

The object description states that altering the contents of the books has a retrocausal effect on reality, with "with memories, locations, physical structures, physical states, and even complete existences changing accordingly to alterations appiled to the books." Any time retrocausality is involved, you're going to run into the Grandfather Paradox - why would you change something that already is?

The test logs and Incident 4001-F play hopscotch with the paradoxes created by retrocausal effects. Some of them imply that the testers are immune to the effect, some don't. 4001-F is the biggest offender, because it demonstrates that the Foundation cannot detect the changes outside of noticing that people had nosebleeds. What's worse, it demonstrates that people making changes aren't immune to the change - whoever made the change also had a nosebleed and was so dazed they were standing around for the seven hours it took the Foundation to react?

The simplest fix would just be to imply that entities within 4001 are immune to the reality changing effects of changing a book (and a note of how the Foundation keeps a backup data archive in 4001 to cross-reference)… but if there's immunity, that would require a rewrite of the testing logs and Incident 4001-F to reflect that fact.

Heck, that could've been an even BETTER punch for 4001-F to establish the consequences of tampering with history and solidifying the Library's in-description-only "personality." [[REDACTED]] walks out of 4001-F, sabotage complete, to find that the Library's execution of the deletion of Dr Peters cascaded into effects that caused THEIR death. They either get detained as soon as they exit 4001, or they instantly die in an agonizing manner as causality rubber-bands back to them once they lose their immunity.

Overall, a no-vote from me. An interesting concept that's hamstrung by how it doesn't address its second- and third-order effects.

This feels warm, inviting, yet powerful. A lot like the library in question.

I think something that hasn't been mentioned much is how well you characterize the library, how alive it ends up feeling by the end. It commands respect, but respects humanity it turn.

The corresponding tale adds a lot to this article, too.

What might improve this article is a log of various books, and what they're noted as. I'd love to see what Dr. Bright's book reads, or Dr. Gears, or anyone from AWCY. You've got the possibility for some really creative documentation logs here, and I'd love to see those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah, this post really got me. Especially with the implication of some sort of progenitor race that messed up big time with Alexandria. Kudos to the author. Wish I could add 100 upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The implication is certainly correct.

It's all confirmed now with the addition of the Alexandria Burning Tale.

In fairness, I'd never heard of the Wanderers' Library until after I'd written this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep,this is my new favourite scp,kudos to the writer for writing this awesome story…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just think of going down there and looking at your family's history, haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the implication I'm getting is that some previous incarnation of humanity fucked up royally, and Alexandria burned them all.

the interview with the Watcher was really heartwarming, and the semi-sentience of the Library was a really cool idea. Overall a good read, solid +1. Though I can't help but feel that it's a bit of a lesser copy of the Wanderer's Library that deals in fact instead of fiction

item.title: Re: My Reason for Downvoting this
item.content:encoded: It makes things too easy under the assumption that the information is safe to use. Perhaps the foundation using the archives for their own means results in severe consequences against them. I think that would be likely with the consequences of trying to play god in the test logs and Alexendria's seeming sentience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm late to the party, I don't care. This SCP is amazing. I don't know why, but the concept, execution and everything made me so immersed that I couldn't focus on anything else but this scip. Bravo, +1

item.title: Re: My Reason for Downvoting this
item.content:encoded:
It seems like with the AI's they have, as long as they know someone's name, or know someone's birthday (but not the other of the two) they could just have the AI search all books under that name, or that birthday, for phrases and keywords having to do with the GOI they are looking for info on.

I suppose it's entirely possible a version of the SCP-verse could exists where most of the GOI's composed of humans don't exist. If it was a verse with Alexandria Eternal in it, the likely reason for the non-existence of said GOI's would probably be BECAUSE the Foundation has the library, and destroyed all their enemies using its knowledge.

Changing to a no-vote. Speaking just for myself, it still feels as if any canon where the Foundation DOES have Alexandria Eternal would just be, well… .a bit boring, I guess? I guess what I mean is that even if it IS guesswork, as long as they have Marvin and Molly cataloguing the books and their contents as stated here, it's still only a matter of time before they find the books for Grand Karcist Ion, various Daevite Leaders, etc.…and then find out everything they ever wanted to know about the strengths and weaknesses of some of the scariest parties to ever be enemies of the Foudation's interests. It just seems too, well… .convenient and easy. No matter what universe's version of the Foundation we are talking about, if they have a pair of AI's cataloguing these books, then we can be absolutely sure those AI's are programmed to flag anything related to GOI's or POI's, so any universe where the Foundation has access to this anomaly would in the end be one with an "all-knowing" Foundation in the long-run.

I think this is something where in the end we'll have to just chalk it up to different tastes. I personally like a version of the SCP-verse where the Foundation is as far from all-knowing as possible, where nothing is ever easy or convenient for them. This skip feels like the universe making things too easy for our "heroes."

item.title: Re: My Reason for Downvoting this
item.content:encoded:
Simple.

There is no one canon.

There may well be a Universe where the Foundation turned Alexandria into a weapon. Or a Universe where they exploited her to her extremes. Or a Universe where access to her wasn't restricted to the most carefully trusted of agents. This is not that canon. There is no guarantees that certain GOIs exist in this canon- indeed, Alexandria pretty deliberately ignores most existing canon.

Furthermore, you need the birthday and an accurate name to use someone's book- which the Foundation doesn't have for most of their foes. Beyond that, blind guessing is not going to get you far with 7 billion active books.

item.title: My Reason for Downvoting this
item.content:encoded:
I apologize this is so long, but I want to explain in-detail my reasons for downvoting this article:

So after first reading this skip, I upvoted it because the underlying concept is amazing and intriguing, and the twist (that the library burned once before) is pretty awesome. However, upon further thought, I had to change my vote to a downvote, for one very simple reason:

+ show block
– hide block

This skip is far, far too overpowered and broken as a source of intelligence for the Foundation. What I mean is, I find it impossible to believe that the Foundation has access to this Skip, and has used an AI to catalogue the books within it to such an extent that they can seek and find the books for particular random D-Class or Foundation Researchers with cancer, and that at the same time the Foundation's enemies would still exist.

What I mean is, the Foundation knows the names/identities, and even birthdays, of plenty of individuals within various GOIs: The GOC, Neo-Sarkic Cults, the CotBG, and of course they know the MAJORITY of names and birthdays of individuals within the Chaos Insurgency due to the backbone of the organization being former Foundation agents who went rogue.

So, why the heck do these various "enemy" groups of interest STILL EXIST if the Foundation has such an easy means of gaining all the intelligence they could ever want on them? Every single anomalous human, every single person-of-interest the Foundation has ever had to think about or deal with, their complete life-story, including all their strengths, weaknesses, and plans, should be easy for the Foundation to find within Alexandria Eternal. Grand Karcist Ion, the person who wrote SCP-140, Robert Bumaro of the CotBG… they should ALL have books there. If this skip exists, then how in the world does the Foundation not know who Dr. Wondertainment really is? Meanwhile, if Alexandria Eternal exists then the Foundation should NOT be at a loss for the backstory and origin of any of the anomalous humans in their custody.

To summarize: I find it impossible to suspend disbelief enough to concede that this SCP could exist while at the same time, enemy GOI's also still exist and the Foundation is still at a loss to explain the origins of many human-born SCP's. It is simply impossible to logically reconcile the two concepts.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Edit: I would possibly change my vote to an upvote if you explain somewhere that the Foundation has only been able to catalogue something like the tiniest fraction (maybe a few hundred thousand) of the books within, and that the D-Class and researcher-with-cancer chosen for testing were books they HAPPENED to find that just so happened to correspond with personnel within the Foundation. The way it is written now, though, makes it sound like when they want to find a particular random D-Class or Researcher's book, they can just sent Marv out and find it at will, quickly. Even if you DO make this change, I still find aspects implausible, due to the fact that the Foundation is AWARE OF THE BIRTHDAYS of plenty of people-of-interests and even some human-born SCP's about which they don't seem to have intelligence on their backstories. But it is impossible that the Foundation could know someone's birthday and not have intelligence on them thanks to this library.

item.title: Re: Addendum 6
item.content:encoded:
maybe some other dominant species

That was my thought. Maybe the Children of the Night or the fae. (The fae could work especially well given SCP-4000.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is such a wholesome, nice SCP with a bittersweet touch. This could work just as well as a 001 entry, and I personally would have liked this as 4000 because it nailed the History part so well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the input. Fixed the former.

As to the latter, I know it's terrible, but I like the name too much to change it.
Ok, so I have been reading this wiki for a year, and I just wanted to say I recently joined the site just so I could upvote this brilliant example of an SCP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two nitpicks-
1. Tone just doesn't feel right to me. Maybe it's the insistent referral of 4001 as "The Library" or "Alexandria Eternal" rather than just 4001.
2. I cannot take the name Lincoln Abrams seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a fascinating and scintillating read. Sorry I missed this during the contest, this is great!

Oh man, this turned out waybetter than it should have.

Thanks to everyone who provided feedback, and everyone who voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was expecting a cooler cognitohazard image! Of course, very minor point.

I've seen this concept before, a lot, including recently in The Magicians.

Despite my dislike of the length, though, it's ultimately the details that makes this for me. My favorite part was the first interview log. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only one that we know of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's my favorite 4000 and it perfectly fits with the idea of history. A history that can be touched. upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one for me is easily the best entry for the contest. It feels like a very impacting SCP that really deserves the 4000 spot. I would personally love to read what might be on my book, or of the people that I know.
Plus the questions it raises are very well thought out. If anything I'm surprised there's only one incident concerning somebody ripping out a page of a book. Easy +1

item.title: Re: Addendum 6
item.content:encoded: The continuous generation of the ash can be interpreted as the Library giving the Foundation a warning about what happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a wonderful, expertly-constructed piece which gradually reveals aspects of its mystery while raising new questions, and coming to an emotionally satisfying conclusion. Easy +1, one of the very best 4k entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whatever 049 is, he sure isn't human, and thus would not have a book.

105, 507, etc on the other hand, do have books.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it’s an interesting idea, it would be cool if there was a book in this library about SCP-049

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the feedback.

At this point, there's only so much that I can feasibly ammend, so I acknowledge this might not reach your preferences. I know it's a bit long and meandering, but that's something other people have appreciated, and I can't really cut much out without performing a full rewrite.

Removed HMCL reference in the security protocols.

Cleaned up researcher notes. Removed the overblown stuff.

Cleaned up grammatical and styling as per notes.

Removed the object reclassification. Moved the monologue down into the final addendum. Since it was originally the final note for Alexandria, it feels a bit better having it back there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to like this, but there's so much here that doesn't really add to base concept after its presented. As a result, I just kind of felt like it dragged on before ending without really going anywhere.

Notes
– hide block

WARNING: HCML and O5 Approval Required

Right off the bat here I have issues, as I don't like the whole HMCL thing (I consider it to be a nonsense relic best left in the past, though obviously this is just a personal taste thing), but you've also mis-spelled it and continue to do so throughout the article. It's HMCL, not HCML.

All of the security and containment procedures for this thing feels massively overblown too. I get that it's a Big And Powerful Thing, but there are plenty of things with far more potential for abuse that don't have mind-destroying cognitohazards and threats of termination.

within the archives. As such, the archives as a whole are largely dark.

"archives" here in both of these cases (and anywhere else in the article) should be "SCP-4000".

Each bookshelf is double sided, each side holding some 8 rows of 5,000 books

No need for the word "some" here.

Notes from Dr. Henricks: Good god. This thing can grant us power over life itself. I wonder what else we can achieve with this?

This reaction feels over the top (as do many of these notes). This result isn't surprising or even particularly powerful considering the scope of some of the other SCPs out there.

"callously stalked, brutally [REDACTED] and slowly [REDACTED] Maria Hermez. (Line located on the second last page of the book.)

You're missing the closing quotation mark here.

"died horribly, agonisingly, and justly from his organs doing what they were always meant to".

These books are originally described as containing the full and accurate accounting of a persons life; these sentences (and all other sentences that are taken from the books) feel very subjective to me. This is just an issue with the original description of the books, as this aspect is explained as being part libraries personality.

I felt like those testing logs went on for probably twice as long as they needed to; very little new or unexpected info came from most of these tests.

Result: [REDACTED]13

Notes from Dr. Travers: Holy shit. We are definitely not trying that again.

This is a horrible trope and I honestly rolled my eyes when I read this entry. Would have been far more interesting to detail what actually happened, which suggests that you just wanted to add some "oh shit" factor. Doing it this way hasn't really worked since series 1.

"Researcher Note: A reminder that following incident SCP-4000-F, no personnel, no matter how highly ranked, is permitted to be alone while in the archives. All it takes is one nutjob to tear out one early page and all of human history breaks. This includes you, O5-Council.

-Dr. Lincoln Abrams, Archive Manager"

Noted before but I felt worth noting again when I read this one, especially because of the jab at the O5 council; all of these notes feel very tonally off and very unprofessional.

The entire of addendum 5 seems pointless to me. I see no reason for the Object Class of this to be changed, as it didn't suddenly become more difficult to contain, so this just seems to serve as an excuse to have weird personal jabs at the original testing researcher. I thought this would continue the whole "personality of the library" angle (which was initially hinted at by the first Watcher they found) but that ended up being the end of it.

I really wanted to like this concept, as I think the Library of Alexandria is a great hook for an article and base concept itself is cool, but I have far too many issues here to do anything but downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ammendments made.

I'm still learning the subtleties of gender dysphoria and lines of management for it. I appreciate the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading through all the contest entries so far, this is by far my favorite. A fantastic SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I'm new to the site, this is my first time commenting. I've been reading for a year or so, and happy to finally be a little part of this awesome community ^_^

I really like this SCP. I have a couple of suggestions about wording though, I'm a scientist so I do a lot of scientific writing, reading papers, proofreading, etc.

+ show block
– hide block

Since some of my suggestions are a little complex in terms of reasoning, this comment is really long :P Sorry about that!

There's a sentence in the Description where I think the wording could be adjusted: "Each book is written in a language which is simultanueously unlike any existing language known to the Foundation, while simultaneously being completely comprehensible to any literate individual reading it."

Firstly, the first use of 'simultaneously' has a typo. Secondly, I feel like the sentence is a little clunky to read with two uses of simultaneously; you could cut out the first use of the word, and it would flow a little smoother.

The second thing I'd like to address is in the Testing Logs, Test subject D-0120.

I'm a trans woman, so I'm really happy that you don't misgender this person, and that you acknowledge the existence of gender dysphoria!

However, there is a part of the wording that I take issue with and think could be rephrased or adjusted: "preferring to identify as a male, and requested gender reassignment surgery."

I've known a lot of other trans people over many years. None of us ever say the words "I prefer to identify as"; it's something cisgender (non-trans) people who are not aware of trans issues say to describe us to other cisgender people who are equally uninformed.

We just tend to say "I am". The "X identifies as Y" narrative was created by cisgender doctors in the mid-20th Century to categorise trans people as our birth gender, not our true gender; they referred to a trans woman as "a man who feels female" rather than just "a woman", as they should. In order to access treatment trans people had to lie to the doctors and pretend to fit the checklist/narrative those doctors had created. Since the media then went to those doctors and other cisgender people when writing about trans people (rather than talking to trans people), those prejudices, terms, narratives and ways of thinking became fixed in the public consciousness.

Hence the transphobic "I identify as an xyz" remarks that many people make about trans people. And how many cis people refer to trans people as someone who 'identifies' as something else.

Since this is in the third person, it is tricky to word this particular sentence. Maybe something along the lines of "D-0120 has since shown gender dysphoria and still recognises themselves as male despite the effect of SCP-4000." would remove the rather nasty connotations of 'preferring to identify'.

Then there's "Gender Reassignment Surgery".

There are a lot of ways to refer to the array of different surgeries trans people can undergo. The oldest is "Sex Reassignment Surgery" (SRS), which has some problems; it reduces biological sex to genitals, when sex is actually a complex interconnected system of primary and secondary sex characteristics, genotype, phenotype, biochemistry, neurology and more. Vaginoplasty/Labiaplasty/Phalloplasty/Metoidioplasty/etc do not change all of a person's biological sex, they just rearrange their genitals into a configuration they are comfortable with. SRS is a reductive oversimplification that gives a false impression of how the surgery works.

Likewise, "Gender Reassignment Surgery" has the same problem; it reduces gender to genitals, when gender is two separate things (socially-constructed gender roles, and a person's internal gender identity. When trans people say 'gender' we're referring to the second one, which is thought to be neurological). If you're interested in more on gender roles and gender identity, Whipping Girl by Julia Serano is an older text but much of it is still correct (though terminology is outdated and it's focussed on trans women and academic feminism).

"Gender Reassignment Surgery" plays into the narratives of transphobes that "genitals = gender", and explicitly misgenders trans people who are pre-op or non-op. I'd argue "Gender Reassignment Surgery" is more problematic and counterintuitive than 'SRS'.

These days more and more people are using the term "Genital Reconstructive Surgery".

This is pretty much the ideal term tbh; it accurately describes the physical procedure, does not misgender anyone, creates no confusion and has no other implications. It cannot be misinterpreted, or twisted to misgender a trans person. It also allows for non-binary people to get it without verbally implying anything about their gender or sex.

So, if you are willing, I would suggest switching "Gender Reassignment Surgery" to "Genital Reconstructive Surgery".


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Alternatively, SCP-113 allows a person to change gender. This could be an opportunity for a crosslink requesting access to SCP-113 to treat D-0120?

Love the SCP overall, a really interesting read! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was originally going to downvote due to issues with clinical tone. Ended up upvoting because the scp is just… good. I would urge you to reread this article and think if its contents would likely to be put down in a lab report to settle tonal issues, but other than that, really solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely loved this SCP, in scope, implications, characterization, the conversion of the Researchers working inside, everything.

As with every mystery, there are multiple interpretations of the final addendum. Here's mine:

The beginning of the Library is associated with the Toba Supervolcano eruption, approximately 75000 years ago. For hundreds of thousands of years before this point, anatomically modern humans had lived, and the Library recorded them all. They had begun to spread beyond the original birthplace in West Africa.

When the Toba Supervolcano erupted, worldwide temperatures dropped dramatically for years. Humans living in northern climes died off in droves. In the depth of winter, humans in modern day Egypt were desperate for warmth. They discovered the library, full of bookcases and scrolls. The library, in its innocence, had no Watcher. The humans used the recently invented technology of fire to warm themselves. In doing so, they accidentally set fire to one section of the Library, which quickly spread to nearly every scroll in the Library, killing nearly every human in the world. Only a single cluster of approximately 1000 humans survived in the long term, and so the Library rebuilt itself, wiser and warier.

item.title: Re: Addendum 6
item.content:encoded:
Prior to reading this, I had two things in my head:

First, that the library made some distinction at some point regarding what a human is leading to a set of parents being considered a "non-human" but their offspring being considered a human. Alexandria emerged with us and alongside us in this interpretation.

Second, the ash is from a previous set of books/scrolls/whatever media. This previous set recorded the lives of a previous set of beings now totally lost to us. Maybe they were more humans, maybe some other dominant species; but whatever they are, they are gone beyond the pale now, their fates unrecorded. Less than dead, they never were.

Of course, these are my own best guesses at what was in your mind and it seems I was wrong, but you didn't show the monster and the possibilities that your writing left open, and what I guessed you might be hinting at, lead to my upvote. I still love it.

item.title: Re: Addendum 6
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, it only sort of makes sense to me too.

This is what happens when you write about retrocausality. Stuff gets weird.

item.title: Re: Addendum 6
item.content:encoded: Well… whatever. It sort of kind of makes sense? I'm too tired to be nitpicky right now. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite the humour and technical writing of the some of the other entries, I must admit this my favourite for -4000. The charm, the ending, but you had me fascinated with a succinct, clear opening that just kept dragging me down the rabbit hole. Only thing I'm sorry about is that I can only +1 once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If an item can be used to heal personnel who are working to contain other anomalies, then it is helping to contain other anomalies, and could be classified as Thaumiel. The reason SCP-500 is classified as safe is because Thaumiel wasn't used until Roget's proposal and SCP-2000, which came after Series 1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the idea but I don't buy the test logs. I doubt that the foundation would allow such testing of something they know to be reality-bending on a worldwide scale. I feel the foundation wouldn't bend reality that way. Couldn't +1 because of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Marv and Molly aren't quite humanoid in personality, much less appearance, but their connection to Alexandria as her librarians has residual effects. Alexandria wants Watchers, and they're the best she's ever had.

item.title: Re: Addendum 6
item.content:encoded:
Gods, you're making me have to actually think about this stuff.

Okay, here's the best degree to which I can rationalise this.

Essentially what happens in a mass CK-class event involving the Library is a 'reset'.

The Library burns, resulting in the sudden deletion of over 100,000,000,000 human lives across history. A few books randomly survived the burning, but the temporal damage would be too immense for them to survive anyway- but their "history" still exists, with the idea of humanity as an intelligent cultural species floating around. The most stable outcome of this is for the timeline to run as though those humans didn't exist in that period of time, which means that proto-humans (not yet registering in Alexandria) spend some 75,000 years longer banging rocks together.

Then, the impact of the residual history kicks in, giving those proto-humans a little boost culturally, and humanity as we know it starts again.

Though explaining this is like providing a solution to the "go back in time and accidentally put your grandpa inside a nuke blast" problem. There's no solution that makes complete sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really really enjoyed this, but there were a couple things that I felt detracted from it.

Firstly, given the internal logic of this SCP, shouldn't some books from the very beginning of the library still survive? If it's an accurate record of all humans, then unless the books that detail some of the first humans still survive, humanity would have ceased to exist.

Lastly, I feel like robots used for a library cataloging system would be less like humanoids (granted I'm not sure if this was the intended implication), and a lot more like the stuff you'd expect to see in an automated Amazon sorting warehouse, with like… drones.. and stuff.

Otherwise, +1 !
